text.title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the rag methodology. Sources: Lewis et al. — Retrieval-Augmented Generation (arXiv:2005.11401); Anthropic — contextual retrieval (2024 engineering blog); LlamaIndex / LangChain RAG documentation.

rule.statement: Chunk documents with explicit chunk-size + chunk-overlap params (e.g. 512 tokens, 50 token overlap); never rely on default chunkers.
rule.rationale: Chunking is the dominant retrieval-quality knob; defaults are tuned for generic corpora and underperform on domain-specific data.

rule.statement: Use hybrid search (dense + sparse / BM25) for any production RAG; pure dense underperforms on rare-term and exact-match queries.
rule.rationale: BM25 catches the rare-token queries that embeddings miss; hybrid is the production default in 2026.

rule.statement: Apply a cross-encoder reranker to top-K (≥ 20) retrieved chunks before feeding to the LLM; never feed raw vector-DB output.
rule.rationale: Reranking lifts retrieval Precision@5 by 10–30 percentage points; the cost is small relative to the LLM call that follows.

rule.statement: Prepend a 1-sentence context header to every chunk (`This chunk discusses X within document Y`) via the Anthropic contextual-retrieval pattern.
rule.rationale: Header-augmented chunks improve retrieval recall 30–50% on multi-document corpora at marginal cost.

rule.statement: Score retrieval (hit-rate, MRR) and generation (faithfulness, answer-relevance) independently; never report only an end-to-end score.
rule.rationale: A high end-to-end score can hide bad retrieval that is masked by a generous LLM; separation localises the bug.
